sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Wayne, presented with a sore on his right foot that has not been healing for two to three weeks.
He had previously seen a podiatrist two years ago but was unable to find a new one after they moved.
The wound started as a blister from a new pair of shoes that he purchased for travel.
He denies any trauma to the foot and has been applying a band-aid to the wound.
He finished a course of antibiotics yesterday but the wound did not improve.
He reports that the wound does not hurt and has not had any other symptoms such as fever, chills, or drainage.
Past medical history: The patient has diabetes and his last HbA1c was a little over 8.
He also has a history of neuropathy.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's temperature is 98.1°F, heart rate is 72, respirations are 16, and blood pressure is 110/65.
Physical exam: The patient has a 1x2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the right fifth MTP joint, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, no fluid collection, no necrosis, and no odor.
The vascular exam shows palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, but no palpable DP or PT pulses, with Doppler signs present.
Test Results: The patient's right foot X-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with possible neuropathy and a history of poorly controlled diabetes.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; possible neuropathy; poorly controlled diabetes.
Plan:
I prescribed collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily and covered with a dry sterile dressing.
I will perform a debridement in the office to remove dead tissue.
I will order an Ankle Brachial Index (ABI) to determine the blood supply to the foot.
I will refer the patient to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates poor blood supply for wound healing.
I will see the patient again in two weeks.
I will also refer the patient to a diabetic consultant to discuss ways to modify their diet to improve their HbA1c levels.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in two weeks to reassess the wound and monitor their progress.
